Biol. 27, 1165–1177 (2020). S. Wang and colleagues revealed that, after the repair of DNA lesions, the epigenetic mark H3K4me2 is deposited to restore protein homeostasis and thus antagonize DNA damage-driven ageing.

Bahar and colleagues demonstrated that cell-to-cell variation in gene expression increases in aged single cardiomyocytes, suggesting that genome damage could trigger stochastic variations in transcript levels, thus compromising cellular function during ageing.

references.author: MA Lodato
references.unstructured: Lodato, M. A. et al. Aging and neurodegeneration are associated with increased mutations in single human neurons. Science 359, 555–559 (2018). Using single-cell sequencing, this study revealed increasing somatic mutations in neurons during human ageing, indicating that even post-mitotic cell types accrue mutations that might trigger age-associated functional decline and degeneration.

references.author: WP Vermeij
references.unstructured: Vermeij, W. P. et al. Restricted diet delays accelerated ageing and genomic stress in DNA-repair-deficient mice. Nature 537, 427–431 (2016). W. P. Vermeij and colleagues showed that premature ageing in DNA repair-deficient mice could be alleviated by dietary restriction, suggesting that DNA repair is capable of reducing DNA damage infliction and promoting genome stability.
